app.name: Spellbook
app.bestFor: Word-native drafting and review co-pilot favored by transactional lawyers and in-house counsel doing high volumes of commercial agreements.
app.rankBasis: Recurs in independent 2026 legal-AI roundups as the Word-native contract specialist, and G2 rates it 4.7/5 from 29 verified reviews.
features: Microsoft Word contract review and redlining
pros: Word-native workflow avoids context switching
users report faster drafting and review with an easy-to-use interface
focused product fit for transactional contract work
cons: no public rate card; procurement is sales-led
not a full CLM for approvals, signing, and lifecycle reporting
not primarily a case-law research platform

app.name: LegalOn
app.bestFor: Attorney-built playbooks in Word for in-house teams that want fast, standardized contract review with pre-approved positions.
app.rankBasis: Appears repeatedly in 2026 contract-review comparisons, while G2 review summaries cite time savings, Word integration, and playbook consistency across its verified user feedback.
features: unlimited AI contract review and redlining
Microsoft Word add-in and web platform
pros: attorney-built playbooks make review standards repeatable
users value faster review and streamlined workflows
Word integration and translation support fit existing legal work
cons: limited document merging and advanced file-management features are reported pain points
some users report friction accessing older translation workflows
no public rate card for team plans

app.name: Harvey
app.bestFor: Enterprise legal AI for large law firms — research, document analysis and contract review at scale via conversational queries.
app.rankBasis: Independent 2026 reviews consistently place Harvey among enterprise legal-AI leaders; Lawyerist rates it 4.4/5 and covers research, drafting, document analysis, and contract review.
features: secure document storage and bulk analysis
pros: broad coverage across research, drafting, review, and due diligence
enterprise-oriented security, collaboration, and integrations
agentic workflows can automate multi-step legal tasks
cons: enterprise sales and implementation make it a poor fit for many small teams
pricing is opaque and commonly described as expensive
lawyers still need to verify citations, legal conclusions, and generated work

app.name: Thomson Reuters CoCounsel
app.bestFor: Research and document review deeply integrated with Westlaw and Practical Law for firms already in the Thomson Reuters stack.
app.rankBasis: G2 rates CoCounsel Legal 4.5/5 from 310 reviews, and independent legal-AI reviews repeatedly position it as the Thomson Reuters option for research plus document work.
pros: authoritative Thomson Reuters content improves research grounding
covers research, document analysis, drafting, and litigation preparation in one product
users praise fast, intuitive legal research
cons: cost can rise substantially when paired with Westlaw or Practical Law
plan boundaries matter; Essentials does not include Westlaw primary-law research
generated citations and legal conclusions still require attorney verification

app.name: Definely
app.bestFor: In-Word review and negotiation assistant for teams handling complex contracts where context and definition-tracking matter.
app.rankBasis: G2 shows Definely at 4.5/5 from 1 verified review, while its official customer footprint is 150+ law firms and in-house teams and independent 2026 guides single it out for complex contracts.
features: AI impact analysis across clauses and schedules
tracked changes, redlines, and issues lists
pros: preserves context in dense, negotiated contracts
Word-native tools reduce navigation and review friction
users report straightforward deployment and useful undefined-term checks
cons: independent review volume is very limited
pricing is enterprise and quote-based
specialized contract-review focus is narrower than general legal research suites

app.name: Luminance
app.bestFor: Pattern-recognition contract analysis across large document sets, strong for due diligence and bulk review.
app.rankBasis: Appears in independent 2026 enterprise contract-analytics roundups, and Luminance reports 1,000+ customers; reviewers emphasize its fit for large-scale due diligence and portfolio work.
features: AI contract generation and workflow automation
pros: strong fit for bulk review, due diligence, and pattern recognition
connects drafting, negotiation, repository intelligence, and compliance
enterprise security, integrations, and dedicated support
cons: pricing is opaque and requires a vendor quote
implementation is heavier than a focused Word add-in
independent reviews note better fit for large M&A and portfolio work than everyday legal tasks

app.name: LEGALFLY
app.bestFor: Privacy-first, jurisdiction-aware review for in-house teams in regulated industries, with Microsoft integration.
app.rankBasis: G2 rates LEGALFLY 4.2/5 from 3 reviews and Capterra rates it 4.6/5 from 8 verified reviews; 2026 in-house comparisons include it, but the independent review base remains small.
pros: privacy-first anonymisation is a clear differentiator
broad intake, review, research, compliance, and procurement workflows
users praise the simple layout and flexible support
cons: independent review volume is small compared with the leading tools
one verified review reports that legal-search results do not always lead to primary sources
pricing is custom and not publicly itemized

skill.name: OpenContracts
skill.bestFor: The most-starred open-source contract/legal-document platform (~1.4k★): annotate, analyze and run LLM extraction over contracts — the closest OSS analog to the AI contract-review apps above.
skill.rankBasis: The leading repo in this niche at roughly 1.4k GitHub stars and 171 forks, with commits landing within days; its docs position it as an open document-intelligence platform rather than a narrow template filler.
features: AI agents and built-in MCP/API access
pros: self-hosting keeps legal documents under the operator's control
extensible annotation, extraction, and citation-graph model
active repository with substantial recent development
cons: production deployment needs Docker and additional security configuration
developer-oriented setup is heavier than a hosted contract-review app
operators must provide and maintain storage, models, and scaling infrastructure

skill.name: docassemble
skill.bestFor: The recognized open-source legal document-assembly platform (~1k★, used by courts and legal-aid orgs): guided interviews that generate contracts, court forms and letters from user input.
skill.rankBasis: A mature open-source leader with 967 GitHub stars, 305 forks, 4,612 commits, and a release updated within the last week; it is also documented as a legal guided-interview and document-assembly platform.
features: guided web interviews with branching logic
PDF, RTF, and DOCX document assembly
APIs, OCR, signatures, and multilingual workflows
pros: mature and actively maintained with a large legal-automation community
highly extensible interview logic and document templates
supports many outputs, integrations, and deployment patterns
cons: production deployments require Docker plus database, storage, and service configuration
implementation quality depends on careful interview logic and integration testing
teams must build and maintain their own jurisdiction-specific content

skill.name: Accord Project (Cicero)
skill.bestFor: Linux Foundation project for smart legal contracts (~0.4k★): templates that bind natural-language clauses to a data model and executable logic. Niche but the de-facto open standard.
skill.rankBasis: A Linux Foundation open-standard project with roughly 250 GitHub stars on Cicero and a distinctive template ecosystem; its adoption is smaller than docassemble but its executable-contract model is unusually specific.
features: Concerto data models and generated types
pros: strongly typed models can validate contract logic before execution
open standard and human-readable templates reduce vendor lock-in
connects legal text, structured data, and executable business rules
cons: small, specialized ecosystem and lower adoption than general document-assembly tools
the text-model-logic architecture has a steeper learning curve
it is not a turnkey contract-review or lifecycle-management application

skill.name: open-agreements
skill.bestFor: Small but on-point niche tool: fills standard legal agreement templates (Common Paper, Bonterms, Y Combinator) and outputs signable DOCX files.
skill.rankBasis: A small but active niche repo with 44 GitHub stars, 8 forks, 675 commits, and updates within days; its value is template coverage and primary-source legal content, not broad community adoption.
pros: practical path from structured inputs to editable DOCX agreements
content is anchored to primary legal sources and explicit review checklists
recent commits and releases show active maintenance
cons: very small community and limited independent review evidence
content is primarily U.S.-focused and jurisdiction-specific
users still need to review generated documents and operate the local toolchain

notes: Contract review is dominated by integrated SaaS products; there is no meaningful Agent Skill (SKILL.md) ecosystem for legal/contract work, so the skill track lists notable open-source legal-automation repos (kind:repo) ranked by real adoption/reputation rather than form factor. This is a genuinely thin OSS niche, so the list is intentionally short (4) rather than padded: OpenContracts (~1.4k★, contract analytics — the closest parallel to the review apps) and docassemble (~1k★, the recognized document-assembly leader) lead, then Accord Project and open-agreements as smaller niche tools. Catala (~2.3k★, law-as-code for statutes/tax) and Blackstone (legal NLP, unmaintained since 2024) were considered but excluded as off-topic to contract drafting/review. MCP servers are excluded. On the app track, several sources (LegalOn, Spellbook, Definely) are vendor blogs ranking their own product #1; app ranking weights cross-source consensus (Spellbook, LegalOn, Harvey, CoCounsel recur across independent lists) over any single vendor's self-placement.
